7 min read
Just now

--

Artificial intelligence is usually presented as a software revolution.
We talk about large language models, generative AI, autonomous agents, machine learning algorithms, and systems that can write, code, translate, analyse images, and support decision-making. The conversation often focuses on what AI can do on a screen.
But the next stage of AI will not be determined by software alone.
Artificial intelligence is becoming a physical infrastructure challenge. Every powerful model requires enormous computing capacity. Computing capacity requires advanced chips. Chips require complex supply chains, specialised manufacturing, energy-intensive fabrication, and advanced cooling systems. Data centres require electricity, water, networks, maintenance, and physical security.
And as AI moves from chatbots into factories, hospitals, vehicles, agriculture, logistics, and homes, it will increasingly depend on robots, sensors, actuators, control systems, communication networks, and intelligent machines.
The future of AI is not only digital.
It is physical.
AI Is Built on Hardware, Not Magic
The recent progress in AI can create the illusion that intelligence is becoming independent from the physical world. A user writes a prompt, receives an answer in seconds, and sees only the final result.
Behind that result is an enormous technical system.
Modern AI models are trained and operated using high-performance computing infrastructure. This infrastructure includes graphics processing units, specialised AI accelerators, memory systems, storage networks, cooling equipment, and data centres operating continuously.
The more capable the AI model becomes, the more computational resources it typically requires.
This means that AI progress increasingly depends on access to advanced semiconductors. The companies and countries that can design, manufacture, and deploy high-performance chips are gaining strategic influence. Semiconductor supply chains are no longer only an industrial concern. They are becoming a central issue for economic competitiveness, technological sovereignty, and national security.
A future AI system may appear as a simple application on a phone or laptop. However, its capabilities may depend on a complex global chain involving chip design, lithography equipment, rare materials, cloud infrastructure, energy generation, and high-speed communication networks.
AI is therefore not detached from industrial capacity. It is deeply dependent on it.
The Energy Challenge Behind Artificial Intelligence
One of the most important questions in AI is no longer only how intelligent systems can become.
It is whether societies can power them efficiently.
Training large AI models requires substantial energy. Operating them at scale also requires continuous electricity. As millions of users interact with AI systems, the energy demand of data centres grows. This creates pressure on electrical grids, particularly in regions where renewable energy capacity, storage systems, and grid infrastructure are still developing.
The challenge is not simply that AI uses energy. Every major technological transformation has increased demand for electricity.
The real challenge is efficiency.
Can AI systems become more capable while using less energy? Can hardware become more efficient? Can computation move closer to where data is generated? Can renewable energy systems support the growth of digital infrastructure? Can data centres reduce their cooling demand and improve heat recovery?
These questions will become increasingly important.
Energy-efficient AI may become one of the most valuable research areas of the next decade. This includes efficient machine learning algorithms, low-power hardware, neuromorphic computing, edge AI, advanced cooling systems, energy-aware scheduling, and intelligent management of data-centre operations.
In this context, sustainability is not only an ethical objective. It is becoming a technical and economic requirement.
The AI systems that consume less energy may be easier to scale, cheaper to operate, and more resilient in a world facing energy constraints.
Why Mechatronics Matters More Than Ever
AI is often discussed as if it exists only in the cloud.
However, the most transformative applications of AI will increasingly appear in physical systems.
A robot in a warehouse must detect objects, estimate distances, plan movements, avoid people, control motors, react to uncertainty, and complete tasks safely. An autonomous vehicle must interpret sensor data, understand its environment, predict the behaviour of other road users, and make decisions in real time. A smart manufacturing system must coordinate machines, materials, workers, quality control, and production schedules.
These are not only AI problems.
They are mechatronics problems.
Mechatronics combines mechanical engineering, electronics, computer science, control theory, robotics, sensing, and intelligent systems. It is the discipline that connects computational intelligence to physical action.
A language model can generate a description of how to pick up an object. A robot must still determine the object’s location, estimate its shape, calculate a safe grasp, control its actuators, compensate for uncertainty, and avoid dropping or damaging it.
The gap between understanding language and acting safely in the physical world remains significant.
This is why embodied intelligence is such an important concept. Embodied intelligence refers to intelligent systems that interact with the physical environment through sensors and actuators. These systems do not only process information. They perceive, move, manipulate, adapt, and learn through interaction.
The future of AI will increasingly involve embodied systems: robots, drones, autonomous vehicles, medical devices, smart factories, agricultural machines, and intelligent infrastructure.
The next frontier is not just artificial intelligence.
It is artificial intelligence that can act.
From Chatbots to Intelligent Machines
The first public wave of generative AI has been dominated by chatbots and content-generation tools. These systems are powerful because they can process language, generate text, assist with coding, summarise information, and support creative work.
But the long-term impact of AI may be even greater when it leaves the screen.
Imagine factories where machines adapt production processes in real time. Imagine agricultural robots that identify weeds, assess crop conditions, and apply resources precisely where needed. Imagine hospitals where intelligent systems assist surgeons, monitor patients, and optimise logistics. Imagine cities where traffic systems coordinate vehicles, energy networks respond dynamically to demand, and public infrastructure predicts maintenance needs before failures occur.
These applications require more than advanced algorithms.
They require reliable sensors, robust control systems, high-quality datasets, cybersecurity, efficient communication, mechanical reliability, and human-centred design.
They also require trust.
A user may tolerate a chatbot giving an imperfect answer. Society will not tolerate an autonomous vehicle, medical robot, or industrial machine making unsafe decisions.
For this reason, safety, verification, control, and accountability will become central parts of AI development. Engineers will need to design systems that are not only intelligent but also predictable, explainable, resilient, and safe under uncertainty.
In the physical world, failure has consequences.
Europe’s Opportunity in the Physical AI Economy
The global AI race is often framed around a few dominant technology companies and countries. However, Europe has important strengths that should not be ignored.
Europe has strong research institutions, industrial automation companies, advanced manufacturing sectors, renewable energy resources, robotics laboratories, automotive expertise, and a growing focus on responsible AI.
Portugal also has relevant opportunities.
The country has renewable energy potential, expanding digital infrastructure, a growing technology ecosystem, and a strong connection to European research networks. Portuguese universities and research centres can contribute to areas such as robotics, smart manufacturing, energy systems, autonomous platforms, control engineering, and sustainable AI.
Rather than trying to compete only in the development of massive general-purpose models, European countries can focus on high-value applications where physical systems matter.
This includes industrial robotics, renewable-energy optimisation, maritime technology, precision agriculture, autonomous inspection systems, medical devices, intelligent transportation, and smart infrastructure.
The goal should not simply be to use AI tools developed elsewhere.
The goal should be to build technological capacity.
That means investing in education, research, advanced manufacturing, computational infrastructure, high-quality datasets, and collaboration between universities and industry.
The countries that succeed will not necessarily be those that produce the most AI hype. They will be those that build the strongest connection between research, industry, energy, and intelligent machines.
The Real Bottleneck Is Integration
The biggest challenge in AI may not be creating a more impressive model.
It may be integrating intelligence into the real world.
A powerful AI system is not useful if it cannot operate reliably in a factory. A robot is not useful if it cannot work safely around people. A smart energy system is not useful if it cannot communicate with existing infrastructure. An autonomous machine is not useful if it fails whenever conditions change.
The transition from digital AI to physical AI requires integration across many disciplines.
It requires computer scientists, mechanical engineers, electrical engineers, control specialists, robotics researchers, material scientists, energy experts, policymakers, and industry leaders.
This is where the next major opportunities will emerge.
The future belongs to systems that combine intelligence with reliability. Systems that combine computation with energy efficiency. Systems that combine automation with human supervision. Systems that combine advanced algorithms with robust physical design.
The AI boom is real. But its future will not be decided only by the quality of a model’s answers.
It will be decided by the quality of the machines, chips, energy systems, factories, and infrastructure that make those answers possible.
Final Thoughts
Artificial intelligence is often described as a new digital era.
A more accurate description may be that we are entering an era of intelligent physical systems.
The most important AI technologies of the coming years may not be the ones that produce the most convincing text or images. They may be the ones that optimise energy networks, support doctors, improve manufacturing, reduce waste, make transportation safer, and help machines work alongside people.
The real AI race is becoming a race for semiconductors, energy, robotics, automation, and industrial capability.
Software will remain essential.
But the future of AI will be built in the physical world.
